name: Michael Frerichs
bio: Michael W. Frerichs (born July 28, 1973 in Gifford, Illinois, pop. ~1,000) is the 74th Illinois State Treasurer, serving since January 12, 2015 and currently in his third term. The son of a Teamster truck driver and an AFSCME-member university secretary, he graduated from Yale University (B.A., 1995), then spent two years in Taiwan teaching English and studying Mandarin at National Cheng Kung University. He returned to Champaign County, founded a technology business, served as a volunteer firefighter, and was elected Champaign County Auditor (2002–2007) before serving in the Illinois State Senate (2007–2015, 52nd District). As Treasurer, he manages approximately $60 billion in state investments, has returned more than $2.4 billion in unclaimed property through the I-Cash program, transformed the Bright Start 529 plan from lowest-rated to Morningstar Gold five years running, and launched Illinois Secure Choice for 1.2 million private-sector workers without retirement access. He is running for a fourth term in 2026 with no Republican challenger — the first time in over 90 years a major party failed to file a candidate for a statewide Illinois office.
prior_office: Illinois State Senator, 52nd District (2007–2015); Champaign County Auditor (2002–2007)

jacket.note: ILSBE Dec 31, 2025 quarterly report: $1,398,007.10 cash on hand. $0 in new contributions since Dec 31, 2025 — consistent with uncontested race. $0 debts. Top donors are Illinois labor unions and Democratic Party organizations. Running for 4th term uncontested (no Republican filed, 2026). Campaign finance is robust but has gone dormant as expected with no competition.

red_flags.label: Running effectively unopposed — no major-party challenger for fourth term
red_flags.detail: No Republican candidate filed for the 2026 Illinois Treasurer race, making it the first time in over 90 years a major party failed to file for a statewide Illinois office. Frerichs also considered running for U.S. Senate (to replace Dick Durbin) before choosing the uncontested Treasurer race. Running without meaningful opposition reduces public accountability for a $60 billion portfolio.

red_flags.label: Refusal to divest from Israel bonds despite protest pressure
red_flags.detail: In September 2025, approximately 50 protesters organized by Boycott Divestment Sanctions-Chicago rallied outside Frerichs' Chicago office demanding divestment from Israel bonds. Days earlier, his North Side home was defaced with paint by a group hours after a protest outside a fundraiser. Frerichs defended the holdings, stating Israel Bonds 'have been stable investments that have generated among the highest returns in the state portfolio' and that Israel has never defaulted in 70+ years. BDS activists dispute that returns justify the holdings given humanitarian concerns.

red_flags.label: ISBI fossil fuel and weapons holdings — activist criticism ongoing
red_flags.detail: A Public I (Urbana-Champaign Independent Media Center) report from August 2025 documented that organizations representing Champaign-area groups traveled to Chicago's quarterly Illinois State Board of Investment meeting to demand divestment from fossil fuel and weapons-related holdings. Frerichs serves as ISBI vice-chair. Activists called state investments 'unpopular and immoral.' Frerichs has not committed to divestment, citing fiduciary duty.

red_flags.label: Sole fiduciary model criticism — political and financial roles not fully separated
red_flags.detail: A March 2024 Top1000funds.com analysis noted Frerichs 'bats away criticism that the sole fiduciary model is outdated,' arguing it is 'possible to wear a fiduciary and political hat.' Critics argue that a single elected official controlling $60 billion in state investments with no independent investment board creates structural accountability risks, especially when that official faces no electoral competition.

red_flags.label: Considered Senate run before choosing uncontested Treasurer race
red_flags.detail: Frerichs publicly contemplated running for the U.S. Senate seat vacated by Dick Durbin before announcing in May 2025 he would seek a fourth Treasurer term instead. The decision to choose a guaranteed win over a contested Senate primary raises questions about electoral ambition vs. public service commitment.

key_votes.summary: Frerichs led successful efforts to eliminate Illinois' legislative scholarship program, under which legislators gave free public university tuition to constituents — widely criticized as a patronage tool.
Frerichs advocated for disclosure requirements for chemicals used in hydraulic fracturing ('fracking') in Illinois, seeking public transparency on environmental impacts.

key_votes.bill: Blagojevich lifetime ban from Illinois office (Illinois Senate, 2009)
key_votes.summary: After Gov. Rod Blagojevich was removed for corruption, Frerichs moved to have Blagojevich barred from ever holding office again in Illinois. The motion was carried unanimously by the Senate.

key_votes.bill: Illinois Secure Choice Savings Program Act (as Treasurer, 2018 launch)
key_votes.summary: Frerichs launched Illinois Secure Choice in November 2018, creating a state-administered retirement savings program for an estimated 1.2 million private-sector workers whose employers do not offer retirement plans.

key_votes.bill: ABLE (Achieving a Better Life Experience) multi-state alliance leadership
key_votes.summary: Frerichs led negotiations for a bipartisan multi-state ABLE alliance allowing parents of children with disabilities to save money without jeopardizing federal disability benefits. His office led negotiations resulting in lower fees for participants nationwide.

endorsements.name: Illinois Federation of Teachers COPE (major donor)
SEIU Illinois Council PAC Fund (major donor)
Associated Fire Fighters of Illinois PAC Fund (major donor)

policy_positions: I-Cash unclaimed property program: continuing to return unclaimed property; total returned exceeds $2.4 billion as of 2025
Bright Start 529 college savings: sustaining Morningstar Gold-rated program, keeping fees low for families
Illinois Secure Choice: expanding retirement savings access for private-sector workers without employer plans
ABLE accounts: continuing to lead national multi-state consortium for disability savings
Fiduciary management of $60 billion portfolio: cautious principal-preservation with returns; over $2 billion in investment earnings under his tenure
Engaged shareholder approach rather than full divestment: uses proxy voting and shareholder engagement on ESG issues rather than selling holdings

career_history.highlight: Manages ~$60B portfolio; returned $2.4B+ in unclaimed property via I-Cash; transformed Bright Start 529 to Morningstar Gold (5 consecutive years); launched Illinois Secure Choice (2018); elected president of NASACT (2022) and NAST (2023); serves as vice-chair of Illinois State Board of Investment (~$26B pension assets)

First Democrat to represent east-central Illinois since 1936. Chaired Senate Higher Education Committee and Agriculture & Conservation Committee. Eliminated legislative scholarship program; pushed fracking disclosure; moved to bar Blagojevich from office (passed unanimously)
Appointed by county board then elected. Became Certified Public Finance Officer — only county auditor in Illinois with that credential at the time
Founded technology business in Champaign County after returning from Taiwan; also ran safety engineering firm for civil structures in US and China

career_history.org: National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan
career_history.highlight: Taught English to students, studied Mandarin Chinese. Speaks Mandarin.

trust_indicators.label: I-Cash unclaimed property returned
trust_indicators.value: $2.4 billion returned to individuals, employers, and nonprofits since taking office in 2015 (up from $60,000 claims/year to record volumes). Illinois holds $5 billion+ total in unclaimed property.

trust_indicators.label: Investment portfolio earnings
trust_indicators.value: Over $2 billion earned for Illinois taxpayers through state portfolio management (cautious, principal-preserving approach) since 2015

trust_indicators.label: Bright Start 529 — Morningstar Gold rating
trust_indicators.value: Five consecutive Morningstar Gold ratings (top category); Frerichs took program 'from worst to among the nation's best' by reducing fees and expanding investment options

trust_indicators.label: No Republican challenger filed — 2026
trust_indicators.value: First time in 90+ years a major party had no primary filer for a statewide Illinois office; Frerichs effectively guaranteed a fourth term with no competitive race

trust_indicators.label: Russia divestment leadership
trust_indicators.value: Led bipartisan national effort to divest all levels of government from Russian investments following Ukraine invasion (2022). Illinois held no Russia-linked investments under his management.

trust_indicators.label: Home vandalized by protesters
trust_indicators.value: North Side Chicago home defaced with paint in October 2024 hours after pro-Palestinian protest over state's Israel bond holdings; incident highlighted political exposure from investment decisions

trust_indicators.label: NASACT and NAST president — bipartisan
trust_indicators.value: Unanimously elected president of National Association of State Auditors, Comptrollers, and Treasurers (2022) and National Association of State Treasurers (2023) by bipartisan peer groups
